# TP53 tumor suppressor in pan-cancer genomics

*Generated by BioAgent · 2026-04-17*

**Research Question:** What are the frequency, spectrum, and functional consequences of TP53 mutations across major cancer types, and how do they influence prognosis?

---

## References

1. **) characterizing 3,281 tumors across 12 cancer types identified TP53 as the single most significantly mutated gene, present across virtually every tumor type analyzed. The MSK-IMPACT prospective sequencing study of >10,000 metastatic cancer patients (Zehir et al., 2017; PMID 28481359) confirmed TP53's dominance in the clinical sequencing landscape, identifying it as one of the most recurrently altered genes across tissue types. Mutation rates vary dramatically by cancer type—approaching ~96% in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (PMID 21720365), ~80–90% in squamous-cell lung carcinoma (PMID 22960745), and far lower in papillary thyroid cancer and glioblastoma with IDH wild-type. The mutational spectrum is dominated by missense alterations in the DNA-binding domain (DBD), with well-characterized hotspot residues (R175H, G245S, R248W, R248Q, R249S, R273H, R273C, R282W), though truncating, splice-site, and frameshift variants also occur. Mutational signatures imprinted onto TP53 loci include UV-induced C>T transitions (skin cancers), tobacco carcinogen-associated G>T transversions (lung), and APOBEC-related patterns, linking etiology directly to mutation spectrum** · PMID: 24132290
2. **). A 2023 pan-cancer study (PMID 36860655) demonstrated that TP53 mutation drives a coordinated transcriptional program of increased proliferation and glycolysis, closely mirrored by specific co-selected copy-number alterations; basal-like breast, bladder, and squamous cancers cluster distinctly around this TP53-mutant/high-aneuploidy axis with consistently worse prognosis. Emerging evidence (PMID 41727427) positions mutant p53 as a "central orchestrator" of tumor immune microenvironment remodeling—sustaining immunosuppression through metabolic reprogramming (lactate/alpha-ketoglutarate accumulation), epigenetic hijacking, cancer-associated fibroblast reprogramming, and myeloid-cell polarization. Intriguingly, this effect is highly context-dependent: TP53/KRAS co-mutation in NSCLC appears to enhance immunogenicity and ICI response, whereas in "cold" tumors (TNBC, PDAC), mutant p53 promotes T-cell exhaustion** · PMID: 41397879
3. **): standard therapies including hypomethylating agents ± venetoclax offer limited and transient benefit, allogeneic stem cell transplantation remains the only potentially curative option, and high relapse rates are the norm. In solid tumors, the prognostic impact is more variable and cancer-type dependent, potentially modulated by mutation type (GOF hotspot vs. truncating), allelic state (monoallelic vs. biallelic loss), and co-occurring alterations. Therapeutically, TP53 has long been considered "undruggable," but recent advances have changed this narrative (PMID 41942427): MDM2/4 antagonists (idasanutlin, siremadlin, navtemadlin) restore wild-type p53 activity in TP53-intact tumors; conformation-restoring compounds (APR-246/eprenetapopt) target specific mutants; PC14586 specifically targets the Y220C mutation (NCT04585750 recruiting); and p53-directed immunotherapy strategies are under active investigation. The comprehensive recent review by Baliakas & Soussi (2025; PMID 40524430) underscores the clinical maturation of TP53 as a biomarker—now formally integrated into risk stratification in CLL, MDS, and AML—and the ongoing challenge of extending this to solid tumors** · PMID: 41303305
4. **) highlighted disparities in tumor genomic profiles by race/ethnicity. Whether TP53 mutation frequency, spectrum, and prognostic impact differ systematically across ancestral populations—driven by differences in carcinogen exposure, germline background (e.g., TP53 p.R72P polymorphism prevalence), or tumor biology—has not been comprehensively addressed** · PMID: 40996301
